features.summary: Amazon reported Q1 2022 net sales of $116.4 billion, up 9% year-over-year excluding FX, at the top of guidance range. The company posted a net loss of $3.8 billion, primarily due to a $7.6 billion pretax valuation loss on Rivian investment. AWS grew 37% to $18.4 billion with strong customer adoption and $88.9 billion backlog. Amazon faced approximately $6 billion in incremental costs from inflation, labor productivity issues, and excess capacity following rapid pandemic expansion.
features.management_outlook: Management expects Q2 to include approximately $4 billion in incremental costs, down from Q1's $6 billion impact. They anticipate productivity improvements as labor stabilizes and see opportunity to better match capacity to normalized demand patterns. Infrastructure spending will grow year-over-year to support AWS growth, while fulfillment and transportation capex will be lower in 2022. The company expects fixed cost deleverage to narrow through the year and capacity will be beneficial for Prime Day in Q3 and holiday season.
features.risks: Amazon faces persistent inflationary pressures from fuel costs, shipping rates, and wage inflation totaling approximately $2 billion in additional costs. The company has excess capacity in fulfillment and transportation networks that will take several quarters to optimize. Macroeconomic uncertainty and potential consumer spending weakness due to inflation could impact demand. Supply chain disruptions and geopolitical events like the Ukraine conflict continue to create cost volatility.
features.key_drivers: Prime membership remains strong with high renewal rates and increased spending per member since pandemic start. Delivery speed performance is approaching pre-pandemic levels with widest selection ever for fast delivery. AWS continues robust growth with enterprise customer wins and infrastructure expansion including new local zones. The company is focused on productivity improvements and rightsizing cost structure as demand patterns normalize after two years of pandemic-driven volatility.
